title: Dollar-Value LIFO
event: Hollis Co. adopts dollar-value LIFO with a base-year ending inventory of $100,000. At the end of year 1, inventory at current-year cost is $121,000 and the price index is 1.10. Compute the dollar-value LIFO ending inventory.

computationPaths.narration: Three moves: deflate to base-year dollars to see the REAL change, identify the new layer, then reinflate that layer at ITS year's index.

memorize.body: Deflate current cost by the index → compare in base dollars → each layer carries the index of the year it was BORN.
Layers keep their birth-year index forever — and once a layer is eaten in a decrement, it never comes back.

questions.prompt: Base $100,000; year-1 EI at current cost $121,000; index 1.10. Dollar-value LIFO inventory = ?

distractors.feedback: That's current-year cost — mostly inflation, not real growth. DVL strips it out: 111,000.
You deflated but never REinflated the new layer. The 10,000 base-dollar layer carries its 1.10 index: 111,000.
